quickInsight: God didn't ask the Israelites to be perfect — He asked them to bring what they had, and together they built something sacred.
keyThemes: Everyone has talents and skills to contribute — the tabernacle was built by ordinary people offering what they could (Exodus 35–36)
Making space for God in your life — the tabernacle was a physical reminder to keep God close, and we need those reminders too (Exodus 40)
The Atonement of Jesus Christ can completely cleanse us — the Day of Atonement (Leviticus 16) points directly to what Christ would do for each of us
Holiness is practical, not weird — Leviticus 19 is basically 'be honest, be kind, look out for people, love your neighbor'
Giving your best, not your leftovers — sacrifices had to be without blemish, meaning God deserves our real effort, not just what's convenient
discussionQuestions: The Israelites brought their best materials — gold, silver, fine fabric — to build the tabernacle. What does it look like for a 14-year-old to bring his 'best' to God? What are you tempted to give Him the leftovers of instead?

Leviticus 19:18 says 'love thy neighbour as thyself.' Be real — who's the hardest 'neighbor' to love in your life right now (a teammate, someone at school, online)? What would it actually look like to try this week?
The tabernacle was designed so the Israelites could feel God's presence. Where or when do YOU feel closest to God? What makes that place or moment different?

On the Day of Atonement, the scapegoat symbolically carried away the people's sins. Have you ever carried guilt or regret that you didn't need to keep carrying? What would it feel like to actually let Christ take that?

Bezaleel was filled with the Spirit of God to do craftsmanship — woodworking, metalwork, design (Exodus 35:31-33). Does it surprise you that God cares about your non-churchy talents too? How might God use what you're good at?
teachingIdeas: Bring a toolbox or backpack full of random tools/items (wrench, paintbrush, phone charger, basketball, pencil). Have each young man pick one and explain how that tool could represent a talent someone used to build the tabernacle — then flip it: what 'tool' do YOU bring to the quorum, your family, or your friend group? The point is that God used every skill, not just the 'spiritual' ones.

Read Leviticus 19:11-18 out loud but replace the ancient context with modern equivalents as you go — 'Don't steal' becomes 'Don't screenshot someone's private message and share it,' 'Don't put a stumbling block before the blind' becomes 'Don't set your friend up to fail or look stupid for laughs.' Let them see that this chapter is shockingly relevant to their actual week.

For the Day of Atonement (Leviticus 16), use a whiteboard or sticky notes. Have each young man anonymously write something heavy they or someone their age might carry — guilt, pressure, comparison, mistakes. Stick them on a board. Then physically remove them one by one and read Leviticus 16:30: 'On that day shall the priest make an atonement for you, to cleanse you.' That's what Jesus Christ does — He doesn't just forgive, He removes it.

Ask: 'If you had to build a place where God would literally show up, what would you put in it?' Let them brainstorm, then show them what God actually asked for in the tabernacle — and point out that the real temple we each build is our own life (1 Corinthians 3:16). What are you 'building' your life with right now?
activities: **Service Build Night**: Pick a hands-on service project that uses different skills — assemble hygiene kits, build something for someone in the ward, or do a cleanup project. Assign roles based on each young man's strengths (organizer, builder, communicator). Tie it back to how the tabernacle was built by everyone contributing their specific gifts.

**Talent Showcase / Skill Swap**: Have each young man teach the group something he's good at for 5-10 minutes — a basketball move, a card trick, how to fix something, a cooking skill, a song. The lesson: God gave Bezaleel the Spirit to do craftsmanship. Your talents matter to God and to the people around you. Close with a quick testimony about how God uses ordinary skills for His work.

**'What Are You Carrying?' Hike or Walk**: Go on a short hike or walk. Give each young man a rock to carry in his hand the whole time. At the end, have them set the rock down and talk about the Day of Atonement — the weight we carry that Christ wants to take. Let them write one thing on the rock with a marker and leave it behind as a symbol of giving it to the Savior.
